turns.text: Hello Charolette, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for asking. It feels a little strange, being the one in the chair instead of the one taking the vitals, but I guess I'm ready. 

It’s just… it’s a lot, you know? Being twenty-three and trying to plan for a baby while I’m barely keeping my head above water with nursing school—it’s exhausting. Most days I’m just staring at the bus schedule, stressing about how I’m going to make these appointments work once I’m actually pregnant. Salem is pretty, but it’s a long walk to the stop, especially when I’m already carrying the weight of what happened before.

That’s the part I don't really know how to talk about. My first pregnancy… it just ended before I could even process it. People say "it wasn't meant to be," but that doesn't stop me from seeing an empty spot in my room every night. I know all the data, I know the labs, and I know I’m labeled "medium risk." I see the doctors looking at my chart and I know what they’re thinking. I just wish they’d see *me* instead of the numbers. I want to be a mom so badly, but I’m scared to death that I’m just walking toward another heartbreak. It’s hard to stay hopeful when you’re waiting for the other shoe to drop.

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

Honestly, I think I expected it to be… comforting. In my nursing textbooks, there’s this idealized version of preconception counseling where the provider sits down with you, holds your hand, and helps you navigate the risks. I thought I’d feel like a partner in my own health, not just a patient file that needs to be managed. I wanted someone to sit with me in the uncertainty and acknowledge that while I know the clinical side of things, I’m still terrified.

In reality? It’s cold. It’s clinical. Because I’m a nursing student, the doctors just dive straight into the jargon. They see me and immediately shift into "shorthand mode," rattling off iron counts and titer levels like I’m a colleague rather than a scared woman who lost her first pregnancy. It’s like they assume because I know what a miscarriage *is* biologically, I don’t need any emotional support. 

I’m sitting there in that drafty exam room, and all I hear is a laundry list of tests and risks. They look at my chart, see the "medium risk" flag, and their eyes just glaze over. I leave feeling like a walking case study, not a person. I don’t need a lecture on hematology; I need someone to tell me it’s going to be okay.
Before trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And how did your emotional state actually compare to those expectations once the process began?

I think I naively expected that if I just did everything “right”—like getting my labs in order and staying on top of my health—the emotional side would just fall into line. I thought I’d feel empowered, like I was finally taking control of my future. I imagined I’d be excited, constantly daydreaming about nurseries and baby names, feeling that warm, glowing readiness that people always talk about.

But in reality? The anxiety is just drowning everything else out. It’s like living under a storm cloud. Instead of excitement, I feel this constant, buzzing terror that my body is going to let me down again. It’s not a "hopeful journey"; it feels more like walking a tightrope. Every time I feel a cramp or notice a tiny change in how I feel, my brain immediately goes to that dark place, analyzing it as a potential warning sign. 

I’m so lonely in this. When I look in the mirror, I don’t see a "mother-to-be." I see someone who is perpetually waiting for bad news. I didn't expect the silence of my apartment to be quite so loud. Being ready, or thinking you're ready, doesn't really matter when you're still terrified that the life you're trying to build is going to slip through your fingers again.
What was missing from the support available to you emotionally at this stage — from professionals, your partner, or your wider network?

That’s the hardest question to answer because the answer is "everything." I’m basically doing this in a vacuum. 

Professionally, what’s missing is basic human validation. I’m treated like a set of test results, not a person who’s grieving a previous loss. I want a provider to look at me—not my chart—and ask, "How are you *really* doing?" instead of just checking off the boxes for my titer levels. They assume my medical knowledge acts like a shield, but it actually just makes me more aware of what could go wrong. I need someone to bridge that gap between the clinical risks and the fact that I’m just a terrified twenty-three-year-old girl.

And the rest? It’s just empty. Not having a partner means there’s no one to share the small, quiet moments of fear with. I don't have a mom or a sister nearby to grab coffee with or just sit with while I stress about the bus or the exams. It’s just me and my textbooks, and let me tell you, a textbook can’t hold your hand when you’re crying at three in the morning. I’m expected to be this professional, self-sufficient student, but I’m starving for someone to just *show up* for me, not as a patient, but as a person.
Before conception, what did you expect to find when you looked for information online or through apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

I think I expected to find community—you know, forums or apps where women shared the real, messy, vulnerable parts of trying to conceive after a loss. I wanted to see people talking about the fear, not just the "how-to" guides on tracking ovulation. I thought I’d find a digital space that felt like a support group.

What I actually found was overwhelming, and honestly, mostly useless. It’s all just glossy marketing and aggressive symptom-tracking. Every app is designed to make you obsessed with your cycle, pinging your phone every ten minutes to tell you to log your temperature or your mood. It’s invasive. And the "information" is usually just recycled, generalized advice that assumes you have a partner there to help you interpret it, or that you aren't already drowning in anxiety.

Most of it feels like it’s geared toward people who are just starting out and don't know the first thing about biology, or it’s incredibly optimistic to the point of being insulting. There’s almost nothing out there for someone who knows the clinical side, is dealing with the trauma of a previous loss, and is doing it entirely alone. I don’t need an app to tell me when to test—I need one that acknowledges how terrifying it is to *wait* for the test. It all feels so lonely.
If you had a pre-existing health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? And what actually happened when you sought that help?

Because I’m a nursing student, I walked into those clinics with very specific expectations. I expected a collaborative plan. I wanted a provider to map out a clear path for me—not just "here are your meds," but "here is how we are going to monitor you, here is how we are going to support your mental health while we manage your risk factors." I thought they would see my medical background as an asset, a way for us to communicate clearly so I could feel empowered and informed.

What I actually got was a gatekeeper, not a partner. They see my history and the "medium risk" label, and they immediately go into defensive mode. They don’t want to talk about the *why* or the *what-ifs*; they just want to give me a stack of pamphlets and a referral for another blood draw. It feels like they’re trying to insulate themselves against the possibility of a bad outcome, which makes me feel like a liability. 

I don't need a lecture on the pathophysiology of my condition—I live with it every day. I needed them to acknowledge that my health is tied to my life, not just my labs. Instead, I left with a handful of requisitions and a sinking feeling that if I end up back in that chair for another loss, I’ll still be just a "case" to them.
Did you feel you had to advocate strongly for yourself, or did the system proactively reach out to support you?

I’ve had to fight for every single shred of attention I get, and honestly, it’s exhausting. The system isn't proactive; it’s reactive, and even then, it’s only reactive to the numbers on my chart, not to me.

There’s no one reaching out to ask how I’m handling the emotional toll or how I’m managing the logistics of these appointments. I have to be the one to push for follow-up questions. I have to be the one to remind them, "Hey, I had a loss before, can we talk about what we’re doing differently this time?" And every time I bring that up, I feel like I’m being "that patient." You know, the one who knows too much or asks too many questions. 

It feels like if I don’t advocate for myself, I’ll just disappear into the cracks of the system. They’ll just see the next patient, and I’ll be left wondering if I missed an opportunity to get the care I needed. It shouldn't be my job to manage my own care while I'm already in such a vulnerable state, but if I’m not the one driving this car, I’m pretty sure it’s going to drive right off a cliff. It makes me wonder—if it’s this hard for me, someone who *knows* how the system works, what happens to the women who don't?
Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

I think I always imagined that the process of starting a family would be a shared burden—that there would be someone there to hold my hand in the waiting room, someone to help me puzzle through the insurance paperwork, and someone to just be a soft place to land when the anxiety felt like it was going to swallow me whole. I expected us to be a team, sitting together during those long, quiet evenings talking about our future, or at least being able to vent about the clinic visits without having to explain why they were so draining.

But that’s not my reality. The truth is, there is no partner. It’s just me. 

Comparing that expectation to the reality is like comparing a photograph of a home to an empty, cold room. It’s not just that there’s no one to go to appointments with; it’s the profound weight of having to process every single decision, every clinical update, and every wave of grief all by myself. When I have to catch that bus alone after a discouraging appointment, there’s no one to call on the way home to say, "That was rough." I have to save that space for my textbooks. It’s a very solitary kind of ache, and it makes the whole process feel so much heavier than it already is.
Were there moments where you felt the burden of preparation fell disproportionately on you? What drove that?

Every single day. The burden feels absolute because there is no one else to share the load. It’s not just the big things, like deciding which clinic to go to or how to pay for the mounting costs of these visits; it’s the quiet, crushing weight of the anticipation. I’m the one who has to be my own advocate, my own therapist, and my own historian of my medical trauma, all while trying to keep my grades up.

What drives it? It’s the sheer reality of my circumstances. I don’t have a partner to split the emotional labor, and I don't have family nearby to lean on when the stress becomes paralyzing. But more than that, it’s the way the healthcare system is set up. It expects a certain kind of patient—one who has a support system, who has transportation, and who isn't already stretched to the breaking point by life. 

When I walk into that clinic, I feel the weight of every single "medium risk" box I check. I feel like I’m running a marathon while everyone else is walking. I’m exhausted by the mental gymnastics of planning my life around a pregnancy that isn't even guaranteed yet. The irony is that my medical training taught me how to care for others, but it didn't give me any instructions on how to survive this level of isolation while I’m trying to bring new life into the world.
If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

I think I expected a sense of acknowledgment—that my file wouldn’t just show a "previous loss" like it was a minor footnote in a medical report. I expected someone to sit down with me and say, "We know you've been through a trauma, and we have a specific plan to help you feel safe this time." I thought there would be some sort of trauma-informed care or even just a conversation about the emotional hurdles of trying again. I wanted them to recognize that I’m not just a person who got pregnant once; I’m a person who had her heart broken by the system, and I need extra care to trust it again.

But in reality? There was nothing. When I went back, it was like hitting a "reset" button. They treated the previous loss as something that had already been resolved by the medical staff at the time. There was no follow-up, no "how are you doing emotionally," and certainly no adjustment to my care plan to account for my fear. It was all just standard protocol: *run the labs, check the titers, move to the next patient.*

It felt like they were just waiting for me to be another statistic. The only "support" I got was an invitation to the same cycle of appointments that made me feel like a case study the first time. It makes me feel like I’m walking back into a fire, and the people in charge of the water are just looking at their watches.
Was there a point where you felt your previous loss was not being taken seriously enough in the context of your new pregnancy attempt?

It’s every time I sit down in that exam room. You can see it in their eyes—the moment they glance at my chart and see "previous loss" listed. It’s like they’ve already processed it as a "closed case." To them, it’s just data. To me, it’s the reason I’m terrified to take a deep breath. 

There was this one appointment, only a few weeks ago. I was trying to explain how the trauma of that first time makes me panic when I see certain numbers on a lab report, and the doctor just cut me off. He gave me this tight, rehearsed smile and said something like, "Well, we’re focusing on the here and now, Charolette." 

I wanted to scream. "The here and now" is built on the ruins of what happened then! How can they ignore that? They act as if my grief is a distraction from the "real" medical work, but my fear is a legitimate part of my health profile. If they don't take the impact of that loss seriously, they’re missing half of what makes me *me* right now. It makes me feel like I have to hide my sadness just to be taken seriously as a medical patient, and that’s a heavy mask to wear. They aren't looking for the person behind the pregnancy; they're just looking for the next problem to solve.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

You know, I’ve been thinking about this walk back to the bus stop every time I leave the clinic. It’s always so cold, and the wind in Salem cuts right through my coat. But the real cold isn't the weather. It’s the feeling of walking out of that building and realizing that no matter what I just shared, or how much I know about the physiology of it all, I am still fundamentally alone in this.

I think the biggest thing I want people to understand is that being "at-risk" isn't just about the numbers on a lab report. It’s about the emotional exhaustion of carrying a secret, fragile dream while everyone else is just living their lives. I’m studying to be a nurse because I want to change the way people feel when they’re in that chair—I want to be the one who offers a steady hand and a real look instead of just a cold, clinical summary. 

I just want to be seen as a woman who wants to be a mother, not just a "medium-risk" chart that needs to be processed before five o'clock. If there’s anything you take away from today, I hope it’s that we are more than the sum of our medical history. We are human beings, and we are usually scared stiff. Thanks for listening—I don't get asked that very often.
